item.title: Include PNG in evacuation plans, Marape requests
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has requested the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the Australian government to include Papua New Guineans into their evacuation plan when required. This follows the escalating conflict between Iran, Israel and the United States (US).
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has requested the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the Australian government to include Papua New Guineans into their evacuation plan when required.

This follows the escalating conflict between Iran, Israel and the United States (US).

As reported in The National yesterday, Israel and the US launched strikes on selected targets in Iran, killing Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and immediate family members.

Marape said due to the absence of a PNG diplomatic presence in the UAE, the Government was requesting foreign consulate assistance from both governments to include PNG diaspora and citizens in their contingency and evacuation plans, if the situation warranted emergency repatriation.

“There is no diplomatic presence in the UAE as yet,” he said.

“Alternatively, the Government will seek assistance from the Australian government through the Resident Australian Embassy in Abu Dhabi to include our citizens in their contingency and evacuation plans.”

He added that “our prayers and thoughts are with our PNG citizens and diaspora for their safety”.

“It is my Government’s hope and desire that an amicable truce is reached between all parties concerned,” he said.

“This is necessary for the total restoration of regional and global peace and security in the Middle East and beyond.”

He will write to UAE President Mohammed Bin Zayed Al Nahyan to seek his support for their well-being as and when the need arises.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Court to rule on governorship row
item.description: THE National Court is expected to make a ruling on Friday on the governorship of New Ireland – while restraining provincial seat MP Byron Chan and Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt from occupying that office. Judge Pauline Bre yesterday in Waigani restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from performing and excising the powers and functions as New Ireland governor until further orders of the court.
item.content:encoded:
THE National Court is expected to make a ruling on Friday on the governorship of New Ireland – while restraining provincial seat MP Byron Chan and Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt from occupying that office.

Judge Pauline Bre yesterday in Waigani restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from performing and excising the powers and functions as New Ireland governor until further orders of the court. 

Chan’s lawyer Alfred Manase had sought a court declaration that Chan was the governor of New Ireland after winning the recent by-election for that seat left vacant by his late father Sir Julius Chan.

Manase submitted that Section 101 of the Constitution clarifies that no MP could represent two or more electorates at the same time.

He added that Section 104 (g) explains that the seat of a Member of the Parliament becomes vacant on his death.

Section 106 provides for a by-election to fill the vacancy.

He argued that Chan was the governor because of Section 17(2) of the Organic Law on Provincial Government and Local Level Government (OLPGLLG).

That issue was determined in Launa v Willie OS 402 of 2004, and Wai v Ambane OS501 of 1998.

Manase said Section 19 dismissed Schnaubelt, who was elected by the provincial assembly, from office as there was no longer a vacancy existing following Chan’s by-election victory.

Schnaubelt’s lawyer Rimbink Pato said there were separate offices under the law, and the matter before the court was for the provincial assembly to decide on.

He said that in Mango v Muthuvel 2019 N7916, whether it was by death or whenever a vacancy arose, the person appointed by the provincial assembly as governor should remain in office.

Judge Bre told the parties that she needed time to go through the submissions before making a ruling.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Middle Fly health workers call on leaders to provide dinghies
item.description: WORKERS from Middle Fly in Western are appealing to the local MP to consider equipping health centres with dinghies to conduct health runs and visits. Obo mission health officer in charge and midwifery nurse Junlai Nawalin said there was an urgent need as this would help both Obo and Bosset health centres in the Fly Zibo local level government to provide the needed health services.
item.content:encoded:
WORKERS from Middle Fly in Western are appealing to the local MP to consider equipping health centres with dinghies to conduct health runs and visits.

Obo mission health officer in charge and midwifery nurse Junlai Nawalin said there was an urgent need as this would help both Obo and Bosset health centres in the Fly Zibo local level government to provide the needed health services.

“The two health centres of Bosset and Obo have been struggling to carry out most health programmes like referrals, drug delivery, maternal and other emergencies due to no proper form of transportation.

“It’s hard to do health awareness, and carry out important programmes like immunisation runs and other health checks in communities.

“As a result, most of our catchment areas are not covered most of the time and then we are forced to approach private boat owners to get the work done,” he said.

The Obo health centre covers five villages of Kaviananga, Komovai, Kasa, Levame and Owa and the Bosset health centre covers Aiambak, Bosset, Manda, Mipan and Kwem. The total population is more than 5,000.

Nawalin said most referrals and very sick patients were also forced to travel by dinghies, making almost a day trip to the nearest hospital in Kiunga in North Fly.

“We cannot keep using private dinghies because each time, we are charged very high rates of K400 to K500 per day for every trip.

“That also makes it harder to reach out when we are short of funds to pay for the boat fares or hire.

“It is impossible doing drug delivery or referrals,” he said.

He said the health centers desperately needed the infrastructure to serve the people.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Teachers urged to fill in forms
item.description: TEACHERS throughout the country have been urged to go to their respective provincial Teaching Service Commission (TSC) offices to fill in forms for the ‘Teacher File and Qualification’ digitising project. TSC acting-chairman Maini Mike Ugaia made this call during the launching of this project in Port Moresby yesterday.
item.content:encoded:
TEACHERS throughout the country have been urged to go to their respective provincial Teaching Service Commission (TSC) offices to fill in forms for the ‘Teacher File and Qualification’ digitising project.

TSC acting-chairman Maini Mike Ugaia made this call during the launching of this project in Port Moresby yesterday.

“That data is very important for any organisation in terms of decisions, budget and everything depend on data and we are very pleased and appreciate the Australian government through Partnership for Improving Education (PIE) programme for this project.

He added that the reason they had this project on board was important because there were four key pillars that needed to be achieved.

Ugaia said the first pillar was integrity where they filtered the admission process and conducting audits to ensure all teacher qualifications were genuine, authentic and verified.

“The second pillar is transparency which we are achieving by establishing an effective appointment database system to manage teacher placements from 2024 onwards.

“The third pillar is sector alignment, where we categorise the workforce to meet modern school structure including Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) alignment for secondary schools and specialised vetting for Tvet (technical and vocational education and training) and Fode (flexible, open and distance education) teachers,” he said.

Ugaia said the final pillar was efficiency, ensuring that they streamlined the entry and exit management for the teachers as they joined or retired from the service.

“The project was started in 2023 to see that we capture the 80,000 teachers’ information online so that when we want to make a decision, the data are just a tap away,” he said.

Ugaia said so far from the last three years they had collected 26,000 teachers’ data from the 80,000.

He said PIE had made the process of data collection and storing more efficient.

Ugaia said from the information collected they had identified that many teachers had been teaching without proper qualifications and were terminated.

He urged the teachers who were teaching without relevant qualifications to be honest and leave the profession.

Ugaia stressed that this was an important project that would assist the teachers and they need to ensure the information reached the TSC headquarters.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Dead dog mistaken for human body
item.description: CITY residents and commuters were briefly gripped with fear and alarm following the discovery of a suspected human body in a bag dumped in a drain at Gordon yesterday morning. Police forensics arrived at the scene and under the curious eyes of onlookers, opened the bag.
item.content:encoded:
CITY residents and commuters were briefly gripped with fear and alarm following the discovery of a suspected human body in a bag dumped in a drain at Gordon yesterday morning.

Police forensics arrived at the scene and under the curious eyes of onlookers, opened the bag.

Upon examination, they confirmed that it was not a human body but a dog that had been killed, placed inside a bag and dumped in the drain.

It so happened that someone first spotted the bag and claimed that it contained a human body.

Police who first attended the scene did not open the bag, allowing speculation to spread wildly.

As more people gathered at the scene, the bag was covered with cloth, further intensifying public speculations.

Some stood in the rain, worried it might be the body of a missing relative.

Within hours, several social media posts were made, and the news went viral across the city, spreading fear and uncertainty before any official confirmation was made.

Police officers secured the area and waited for the forensics team to arrive.

When forensic officers eventually opened and examined the bag in full view of the crowd, it was established that the remains were not human but that of a dog.

The confirmation brought relief to many.

Police urged the public to refrain from sharing unverified information online and to allow police to investigate and confirm facts before drawing conclusions.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PM condemns airport vandalism
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has condemned the recent vandalism at Wewak’s Boram Airport in East Sepik, calling it a betrayal of people that such public infrastructure is meant to serve. Speaking ahead of his visit to launch major developments at Boram Airport, Marape said public assets, including airports, roads, schools, health facilities, and police establishments belong to the people and must b
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has condemned the recent vandalism at Wewak’s Boram Airport in East Sepik, calling it a betrayal of people that such public infrastructure is meant to serve.

Speaking ahead of his visit to launch major developments at Boram Airport, Marape said public assets, including airports, roads, schools, health facilities, and police establishments belong to the people and must be protected.

“These public assets belong to you – airport infrastructure, public roads, educational infrastructure, health infrastructure, even police infrastructure are yours to start with. So protect and keep them safe,” he said.

Marape confirmed that law enforcement authorities have been instructed to investigate and take action against those responsible for the vandalism.

Despite the setback, he reaffirmed the Government’s commitment to development in East Sepik, including the planned extension of Boram Airport and construction of a new terminal.

“We will do an airport extension and a brand-new terminal. This remains a good place for commerce, trade and economy. Not just agriculture, fisheries and forestry, but more importantly tourism.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Woman balances motherhood and beauty business
item.description: KELLIANNE Ewam is balancing time between motherhood, a full-time career in marketing and running a business called Willions Boutiques. Ewam, 34, from Morobe, and the only female in a family of eight, is starting a nail technology service through her beauty business.
item.content:encoded:
KELLIANNE Ewam is balancing time between motherhood, a full-time career in marketing and running a business called Willions Boutiques.

Ewam, 34, from Morobe, and the only female in a family of eight, is starting a nail technology service through her beauty business.

She had wanted to become a television presenter or journalist but ended up in a marketing career.

She has an Australian degree in international marketing and has worked for more than 12 years as a marketing professional.

Before starting her nail technology business, Ewam does business at home during her free time.

She did online sales of fashion items, mainly babies’ and ladies’ accessories, on lay-by terms depending on demand.  

Ewam recently started making skin care essential oils moisturisers and said she believes in looking good and feeling good.

“The way I look influences how I feel,” she said.

“I am a self-starter, I always look for opportunities to be better than my former self. Life has challenged me in so many ways, and I believe it has also motivated, inspired me to venture into business.”

Ewam discovered nail technology around 2021 in Wanchai, Hong Kong by a Chinese Malay nail technician. Layer, she tried out services at salons and spas in Port Moresby.

She bought her own self-kit from overseas and began practising.

“So I was self-taught, with the experiences I have here in PNG and overseas have gave me confidence to venture,” she said.

It was Ewam’s husband who encouraged her to venture into nail technology service.

 “I am always grateful if it wasn’t for him, we will never come this far,” she said.

The business was registered in February 2024. After almost a year of mobile services, she signed a lease with Monian Limited for an SME space inside Garden City, Boroko. Today, her customers range from 10-year-old girls to women over 50. Despite the challenges and the competition from other SMEs, Ewam focuses on customer satisfaction.

“I don’t worry about competition, I worry about where or not my customers are satisfied and their Kina pays for quality,” she said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Water PNG gets K220mil loan to expand water supply
item.description: THE Asian Development Bank (ADB) is giving Water PNG Limited a US$60.9 million (about K220 million) loan package to expand and modernise water supply and sanitation services in Papua New Guinea. Water PNG Limited chief executive officer James Young said that the investment was a demonstration of confidence in the government’s Reset@50 strategy to correct the past and secure the future.
item.content:encoded:
THE Asian Development Bank (ADB) is giving Water PNG Limited a US$60.9 million (about K220 million) loan package to expand and modernise water supply and sanitation services in Papua New Guinea.

Water PNG Limited chief executive officer James Young said that the investment was a demonstration of confidence in the government’s Reset@50 strategy to correct the past and secure the future.

“This investment is the ADB’s first water sector loan to PNG in over 25 years, signalling renewed confidence in the country’s water service transformation agenda,” Young said.

“The Government has made it clear that State-owned enterprises have to move onwards and not dwell in failures of the past to keep pace with a growing population.

“We have our plans to replace water and sewage infrastructure that is half a century old and we have the full support of the government and partners to deliver on this rehabilitation,” he said.

The financing package comprises US$30 million (about K124 million) in standard capital resources, US$30.9 million (about K128.1 million) in concessional resources, US$3 million (about K12.4 million) in counterpart funding from the government, and an extra US$1.5 million (about K6.2 million) in technical assistance.

Young said that the funds would facilitate digital systems integration, strengthen institutions and explore public–private partnerships.

“This investment marks a transformative moment for our urban communities,” he said.

“Access to safe, reliable water is a fundamental human right, and the ADB’s support allows Water PNG to fast-track long-overdue upgrades to our systems.

“We welcome ADB’s confidence in Water PNG, especially its commitment to helping us modernise through digital integration, institutional strengthening and exploring innovative partnerships.”

Meanwhile Young said this funding sets the foundation for a more resilient, efficient and sustainable water future for the country.

He said that the Urban Water Supply and Sanitation Security and Resilience Improvement Project was projected to benefit over 160,000 Papua New Guineans.

“Planned upgrades involve rehabilitating wastewater systems, enhancing piped water access, and bolstering utility operations to improve public health,” he said.

“Water PNG is dedicated to ensuring successful implementation aligned with national WaSH goals and the Government’s Medium-Term Development Plan.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Tisa Bank to launch Chinese Yuan account
item.description: FOREIGN Affairs Minister Justin Tkatchenko says the move by Tisa Bank to launch the Chinese Yuan Account is in line with diplomatic efforts by the Foreign Affairs Department.  He said that the move to offer direct settlement for the currency was good news for the economy.
item.content:encoded:
FOREIGN Affairs Minister Justin Tkatchenko says the move by Tisa Bank to launch the Chinese Yuan Account is in line with diplomatic efforts by the Foreign Affairs Department. 

He said that the move to offer direct settlement for the currency was good news for the economy.

“This is also to make business transactions easier for its customers,” he said.

Tkatchenko said that China was the biggest economic and trading partner and the initiative would further facilitate the relationship.

“This is among efforts that the Department of Foreign Affairs was putting in place for the benefit of both PNG and China,” he said.

“The Chinese business community in PNG is also pleased with this service as it will make it more accessible, cheaper and easier for them in terms of procurement and investments.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Mama Bank, Nakanai authority to help women
item.description: THE Women’s Micro Bank (Mama Bank) and the Nakanai development authority have signed an agreement to help women in small-medium enterprises (SMEs) access finance. Nakanai MP Francis Maneke said women and youths in the district should have access to banking facilities and have their own accounts with affordable and adequate access to funds to enable them to play a more active role in the economic d
item.content:encoded:
THE Women’s Micro Bank (Mama Bank) and the Nakanai development authority have signed an agreement to help women in small-medium enterprises (SMEs) access finance.

Nakanai MP Francis Maneke said women and youths in the district should have access to banking facilities and have their own accounts with affordable and adequate access to funds to enable them to play a more active role in the economic development of the district.

He said during the signing ceremony in Port Moresby last week that the Mama Bank would use the previous funding of K500,000 and a further K500,000 for the purpose of MSME and SME access to finance funding.

Mama Bank chief executive officer Gunanidhi Das said the Mama Bank would provide financial inclusion with account opening, easy and affordable savings products, appropriate and easily accessible small loans to informal entrepreneurs to start or improve existing small income-generating business enterprises.

Nakanai DDA chief executive officer Jeffery Osa said the programme would support “mothers selling fresh market produce in the markets in Nakanai District and markets on the road side, simple people”.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: New coach promises fresh era for Lahanis
item.description: NEW Bintagor Goroka Lahanis head coach Michael Moses has made it clear that the next chapter for the Lahanis in the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup will be built on transparency, accountability and fairness. He said these values are needed to restore pride in one of the country’s pioneer rugby league franchise.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Bintagor Goroka Lahanis head coach Michael Moses has made it clear that the next chapter for the Lahanis in the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup will be built on transparency, accountability and fairness.

He said these values are needed to restore pride in one of the country’s pioneer rugby league franchise.

“For six years we have been making up the numbers in the competition, even though we had many talented players,” Moses told The National.

“Now it will be different. I will ensure transparency, accountability and fairness in this team.

“Eastern Highlands is bigger than any of us; the Lahanis flag must again fly high.”

Moses also linked the club’s future to Papua New Guinea’s planned entry into the National Rugby League in 2028 as the PNG Chiefs.

“We are entering the NRL in 2028 as the PNG Chiefs. My focus this year will be on pathway development and winning the Cup,” he said.

Moses believes the Lahanis could play a key role in preparing players for the national franchise and eveloping young talent and building a winning culture will be his top priority.

“This team is bigger than any one of us. It represents our culture, heritage and our community as a whole. I want the team to serve its purpose and make Eastern Highlands proud.”

The reference to exporting players highlights Moses’ ambition to see Lahanis talent move on to bigger competitions, including opportunities similar to those enjoyed by the PNG Hunters in the Hostplus Cup.

The coach admitted the road ahead will not be easy. Strong competition, internal rebuilding and high expectations will all test the team this season.

“There will be obstacles and challenges along the way, but by the grace of God we will not back down,” he added.

“We will outsmart and outrun whoever tries to take us down (this season).”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Weather halts PNG-Fiji clash, American Samoa into semis
item.description: THE Fifa Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers match between PNG Bilums and Fiji was called off yesterday due to adverse weather conditions in Ba, Fiji. Details regarding the rescheduling will be announced later.
item.content:encoded:
THE Fifa Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers match between PNG Bilums and Fiji was called off yesterday due to adverse weather conditions in Ba, Fiji.

Details regarding the rescheduling will be announced later.

Meanwhile, American Samoa made it two wins from two and booked their place in the semi-finals of the Fifa Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers with a narrow 1-0 win over Samoa in Honiara.

Having stunned the Solomon Islands on Friday, Amanda Cromwell’s side knew three more points would virtually secure progression to Round 3, and they delivered.

Mia Toeaina came close early with a shot tipped over by Angelique Tuisamoa, while Alayna Fuamatu-Maafala struck the post with a header.

The breakthrough arrived on the stroke of half-time when Toeaina rounded the goalkeeper and squared for Morgan Patea to finish from close range.

Samoa improved after the break, dominating possession in American Samoa’s half, but failed to convert pressure into goals, leaving their qualification hopes hanging by a thread.

Elsewhere, New Caledonia revived their campaign with a 2-1 victory over Vanuatu at a rain-soaked Govind Park in Ba, Fiji, yesterday.

Both sides needed a win after heavy opening defeats, and New Caledonia struck twice in the first half.

Kané Dralu opened the scoring with a superb solo effort in the 14th minute, before Leana Hmae doubled the lead with a header from a corner in the 34th.

Vanuatu, still chasing their first goal of the qualifiers, saw Nettie Kalsau denied by an offside flag and the crossbar.

Leimata Simon finally pulled one back in the 82nd minute, but it proved too late as New Caledonia held firm for their first Fifa Women’s World Cup qualifying win since 2018.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Ex-Muruk credits pioneers
item.description: THE Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, Papua New Guinea’s premier semi-professional rugby league competition, traces its roots back to 1990 when visionary administrators set out to shape the future of the game, a former PRK Mendi Muruks player and assistant coach has said. Thomas Undupie named the pioneers of the semi-professional competition as former Mendi Rugby League and PNGRFL president Joe Keviame (dec
item.content:encoded:
THE Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, Papua New Guinea’s premier semi-professional rugby league competition, traces its roots back to 1990 when visionary administrators set out to shape the future of the game, a former PRK Mendi Muruks player and assistant coach has said.

Thomas Undupie named the pioneers of the semi-professional competition as former Mendi Rugby League and PNGRFL president Joe Keviame (deceased), Kevin Murphy (deceased), John Numapo, and others “who believed in building a pathway for rural talent to shine on the national stage”.

“Their dream was simple yet ambitious: create a competition that would expose raw talent from across PNG and provide opportunities for players to progress into Queensland leagues and, eventually, the NRL.

“More than three decades later, that vision remains central to PNG’s rugby league identity,” Undupie said.

He said while some argue that PNG should now focus on the NRL Bid Academy in preparation for the PNG Chiefs’ entry into the NRL in 2028, “seasoned rugby league veterans insist the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup must remain the heart of the game”.

He warned that the Chiefs are likely to recruit overseas talent, leaving local players sidelined unless the domestic competition continues to thrive. Undupie said despite its longevity, the DEM Cup competition faces challenges.

“One key policy requires franchises to recruit 75 per cent of their players from the host province and the remainder from elsewhere. Yet, compliance has been inconsistent, with sponsors and owners often prioritising results over provincial representation.”

He said honouring this policy is vital to maintaining community identity and grassroots development. Commenting on the Mendi Muruks, Undupie said backed by Petroleum Resources Kutubu (PRK), they stand as one of the competition’s most enduring franchises.

“PRK has supported the Muruks for 13 years, but despite strong backing, the team has struggled to convert investment into consistent premiership success.

“After breaking a 13-year drought to reach last year’s grand final, familiar weaknesses resurfaced, raising questions about management and player development.”

Undupie called on the PRK board to take a more hands-on role in ensuring the Muruks not only chase titles but also produce representative players year after year.

“The Muruks’ future lies in harnessing talent from the Southern Highlands,” he said, adding that last year, eight affiliated leagues were officially recognised under the PNGRFL: And they include Mendi Rugby League; Lai Valley Rugby League; Nipa Rugby League; Aiya Rugby League; Kagua Rugby League; Erave Rugby League; United Imbongu Rugby League; and Pangia Rugby League.

He said it was likely that Sembiringi and Ialibu would join this year.

The Mendi Muruks trial matches for this year would be held in Lae, Morobe.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Career rebuild for Balimo lad
item.description: AT just 23 years old, Malachai Wakasa has already experienced the highs and lows of rugby league. The lanky outside back from Balomo in Western’s Delta Fly area is now on a mission to rebuild his career and return to the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup (DEM Cup).
item.content:encoded:
AT just 23 years old, Malachai Wakasa has already experienced the highs and lows of rugby league.

The lanky outside back from Balomo in Western’s Delta Fly area is now on a mission to rebuild his career and return to the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup (DEM Cup).

Like many young boys in Western, rugby league was more a way of life.

Wakasa began his career in 2017 while in Daru. In 2019, he moved to Port Moresby seeking better opportunities, joining POM Waliya in the Port Moresby North-West competition. His tall frame, speed, and athleticism quickly made him a standout.

Standing at 189cm and weighing 84kg, Wakasa’s physical tools matched his ambition. His hard work paid off in 2023 when he debuted in the DEM Cup with Gulf Isou.

“It was a proud moment for me and my family and also a great experience,” Wakasa told The National.

That season gave him belief in bigger opportunities, including a shot at the SP PNG Hunters and even national duties. But just as his career gained momentum, injury struck. The 2024 season became a year of frustration, forcing him to step away from the game he loves.

For many young players, such setbacks can end careers. But Wakasa chose to fight back.

Now fully recovered, the 23-year-old is rebuilding his body and confidence.

“I still have the speed, the athleticism and the power,” he said. “I just have to stay committed while making my way back up.”

Currently not attending school due to personal reasons, Wakasa plans to return next year.

In the meantime, he is fully focused on his rugby league comeback.

His first major step came this year at the inaugural Goilala Rugby League 9s tournament, where he featured for the Apete Rats. His team went all the way, winning the Cup grand final.

“For me, that was a big achievement,” Wakasa said.

“It gives me confidence and something to stand on as I push to get back into the DEM Cup competition.”

With the proper DEM Cup season yet to start, Wakasa is keeping fit by playing in off-season competitions while waiting for opportunities.

“I’m just putting my eye out for which teams are interested in me.

“I’m ready to play and maintain my fitness, ready for any possible call-up from the franchises.”

Beyond that, his ultimate dream remains alive: wearing the Hunters jersey and one day representing Papua New Guinea.

Wakasa draws motivation from his PNG rugby league role model, Edene Gebbie. Like Gebbie, he believes speed, discipline, and hard work can open doors beyond local competitions.

The road back to the DEM Cup will not be easy. Competition is strong, and opportunities are limited. But Wakasa believes his best rugby league is still ahead of him.

“I believe in myself and I will get back there soon.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pay attention to the details
item.description: THE motor vehicle engine appears to the common person a very sophisticated machine only because it is made of so many simple parts. Watch a mechanic as he disassembles the monster engine and by and by you see in neatly filed heaps lots of screws, nuts, bolts, plates, steel rods, bearings, casings, and rubber fittings.
item.content:encoded:
THE motor vehicle engine appears to the common person a very sophisticated machine only because it is made of so many simple parts.

Watch a mechanic as he disassembles the monster engine and by and by you see in neatly filed heaps lots of screws, nuts, bolts, plates, steel rods, bearings, casings, and rubber fittings.

The biggest aircraft carrier in the world and the most sophisticated aircraft are made up of the same small or simple parts, only in greater quantity.

You need to add the right levels of oil, of fuel, of electricity and turn the ignition to kick start the monster beast and you are in business.

If the parts are not properly alligned or one part is out of kilter, it is most often the case the machine will not operate properly or at all.

Now, apply the make-up of a machine to the make-up of a society and one should see the exact same principle in operation.

A family, a village, a school, a company, a provincial government or the national government are organisations in every bigger arrangement but made up of many individual human persons performing their designated roles.

What is crucial for the proper working of a school or a warship comes right down to the individual part operating as it should – playing it’s part.

If each is properly in place and performs its tasks well, the machine or organisation should operate in tip top efficiency.

If, on the other hand, a nut or bolt comes unstuck or a teacher is not at work teaching, their missing contributions will impact the mighty workings of the ship or the school by and by.

The whole, it is said, is the sum of its parts.

If the parts are deficient, corrupted or ineffective, the whole will be affected adversely.

And so it is with the workings of the. government.

Many times blame is passed for fault upon the head and for the most part such blame is properly directed.

The head must bear total responsibility, is everybody playing their part?

Papua New Guinea seem adept at passing blame and taking none themselves.

Today, there are too many people who see the spec of dust in somebody else’s eyes, as Christ points out in the good book, but they cannot see the plank blocking their own eyes.

There is another aspect that runs parallel and to this line of enquiry that warrants mention here too.

Small amounts of money, spent here and there without much thought leads to huge draining of the treasury and one cannot quite place where it has gone at the end of the day.

A dam with many leakages will empty by and by unless patched

We have watched with much concern the increasing number of trips abroad during the term of the Marape government by large entourages to many parts of the world with very little justification for both the size of these delegations or the net benefits to the nation.

What is positively astounding is that the same government and leadership that sponsors these trips abroad has the presence of mind to say that there is no financial crisis when the evidence presented by increased borrowings and reports from the Finance Department; by the lack of timely release of warrants from Treasury Department to departments and agencies for goods and services and by the hardships faced by the average citizen tell quite the opposite story.

We have seen, for instance, very many investment missions abroad, but there seems to be no new foreign direct investment planted in PNG yet.

The point is that these trips abroad cumulatively take tens of millions of kina without delivering anything worth that kind of expenditure for the benefit of citizens.

Another wasteful expense that leaks money like a bad tap is the use of hire car business.

Whenever there is a government operation, such as a state of emergency, the hire car business takes up the bulk of the emergency money.

From the expense alone the police and defence could add a full fleet of permanent vehicles to their fleet for long term use but nobody seems to have that on the planning books

Questions have always circulated about who owns the hire car companies, whether the money is spent on friends and political cronies or on legitimate businesses.

Office rent is another area where money is disappearing in drips and drops.

It is preposterous that the State owns both the Sir Manasupe Haus and the Morauta Haus next to each other in Waigani and without refurbishing them is happily paying private investors to house important offices of government.
